summary: Gruber provides a one-week update on Dithering, his new subscription podcast with Ben Thompson. He addresses early feedback including splitting unified podcast feeds into separate ones, explains why they won't offer a free sample episode, and highlights the show's 15-minute format as a differentiator. The post celebrates over 5,000 sign-ups with zero refund requests and shares the origin story of the podcast's name. It's essentially a promotional update framed as a response to listener questions and feedback.
key_points: The unified Stratechery/Dithering podcast feed was the top complaint and was quickly separated into discrete feeds
Rather than offering a free sample, they point to existing free content and emphasize the low $5/month price with easy cancellation
The strict 15-minute episode format appeals both to fans of long podcasts and those who prefer short ones
Over 5,000 subscribers in the first week with zero refund requests signals strong product-market fit
The subscription model is built on the open web through Stratechery's back end rather than locked into a proprietary platform
money_quotes: Ben and I are in relatively uncharted territory here with Dithering's subscription-based model.
Technically, this is very clever, and it should, in theory, work great. But in practice, podcast apps just don't expect one feed to contain episodes from multiple shows.
We've had just over 5,000 sign-ups so far and not one single request for a refund. Zero!
People who like my podcast seem to enjoy Dithering. But a lot of people who don't like The Talk Show like Dithering too — if what they dislike about The Talk Show is that episodes are so long.
The 15-minute hard-and-fast episode length for Dithering gives it a very different feel.
It's really very clever — and completely built on the open web.
key_insight: A paid podcast with a strict 15-minute format and open-web infrastructure can find a substantial audience by solving real listener frustrations with existing podcast conventions.
